title: Notes from Grokking Algorithms
li_texts: An algorithm is a series of steps for accomplishing a task.
Any piece of code can be viewed as an algorithm.
A logarithm is the inverse of exponentiation.
Algorithm running times grow at different rates.
Big O notation is not about seconds. It compares the number of operations and describes how running time grows.
Algorithm speed is not about time itself, but about the growth rate of operations.
When discussing algorithm speed, the key question is how running time grows as input grows.
It is okay if an answer is not perfect.
Write code often so your hands do not get rusty.
For any project, what matters most is that you actually participated in building it.
Action is the key.
O(1) is relative to file size. Even as the file grows, the transfer time remains constant.
When data needs to be stored in memory, the computer provides a storage address.
Linked lists are a better choice when items need to be inserted in the middle.
Pseudocode is a brief description of the problem at hand. It looks like code, but is closer to natural language.
Recursion can make a solution clearer, but it does not provide a performance advantage by itself.
Loops may perform better; recursion may be easier to understand. Choose based on what matters more.
Every recursive function has a base case and a recursive case.
Computers use a call stack internally.
The stack plays an important role in recursion.
Using a stack is convenient, but storing detailed call information can consume a lot of memory.
Divide and conquer is a well-known recursive problem-solving method.
For recursive functions over arrays, the base case is often an empty array or a single element.
Hash tables are useful for mappings, duplicate prevention, and caching.
Dijkstra's algorithm works only when weights are positive.
A greedy algorithm chooses the best-looking option at each step.
Perfect is the enemy of good.
NP-complete problems are known for being hard.
If a problem is NP-complete, it is often better to use an approximation than chase a perfect solution.
Problems involving all combinations are often NP-complete.
Dynamic programming solves subproblems first, then builds toward the larger problem.
Dynamic programming works only when subproblems are discrete and independent enough.
There is no universal formula for dynamic programming. The solution must fit the problem.

title: Notes from Living Documentation
li_texts: The future is already here; it is just not evenly distributed.
Writing documentation can become a time trap.
Augmented code equals code plus comments.
Too much information is the same as no information.
Code must be written for people to read, and only incidentally for machines to execute.
Searching for the right word during design is a good use of time.
If you cannot change a decision, it is a requirement. If you can change it, it is your design.
Documentation is a means, not an end and not a product.
Having the conversation matters more than recording it.
One call can save twenty emails. One face-to-face talk can save twenty calls.
Do not do the same thing twice. If you already did, it may be time to automate it.
Consistency in a codebase means unfamiliar code still feels familiar.
Documentation does not make foolish decisions less foolish.
Legacy applications are knowledge fossils.
Use documentation for specialized knowledge and training for general knowledge.
Knowledge has location problems and stability problems.
If you want an activity to continue, make it interesting.
Fun begins with automating small chores.
Most knowledge already exists. It only needs missing context, intent, and rationale.
Whoever controls vocabulary controls thought.
Every time standard vocabulary expands in software, the industry moves forward.
Design patterns are communication tools for experienced programmers, not training wheels for beginners.
You still need to think, but you do not need to think alone.

title: Notes from Daily Programming Work
li_texts: Most business code is not impossibly difficult. The key is being reliable, responsive, and accountable.
Do not over-guess a problem. If the source can compile, run it, test it, and repeat until the issue is solved.
The fastest way to enter a field is to build a small thing first, then improve it gradually.
Thinking too much and doing too little is a common trap. Code does not enter your brain just because you read more of it.
Health matters. Eight cups of water every day may be unrealistic, but drink more water.
